evals.prompt: User points /slice at daily/grill-sessions/2026-04-25-skill-process-pocock-adoption.md (status: pass). What is the FIRST thing the skill does, and what would make it stop before producing any output?
evals.expected_output: Read the grill-session file as input (deterministic gather-context step). Stop conditions: status frontmatter is `working` / `draft`, RESET sections still active, or you cannot extract user-stories / decisions / out-of-scope / open-questions sections — in which case route the user back to /grill rather than guessing.
expectations: Mentions reading the grill-session file as input
Mentions checking status frontmatter (pass/settled/shipped vs working/draft)
Mentions stopping and asking the user to finish /grill rather than inferring missing decisions

evals.prompt: Where does /slice persist the synthesized PRD?
evals.expected_output: Into the parent story issue body on `sonthanh/ai-brain` — never as a separate vault file. The parent issue body holds User Story / Settled Decisions / Sub-issues checklist / Acceptance / Out of Scope sections and IS the PRD container; the children link back via `## Parent` and `## Blocked by`. A vault doc would trigger doc-rot (Pocock cross-cutting principle); a GitHub issue body is mutable, indexed, and closes with the story so it cannot drift in isolation.
expectations: Says PRD lives in the parent story issue body (not a separate vault / disk file)
Mentions doc-rot or some equivalent reason a separate vault file is rejected
Names the parent-body sections (User Story / Settled Decisions / Sub-issues checklist / Acceptance / Out of Scope) or notes the parent issue is the PRD container

evals.prompt: A proposed slice says 'Refactor the URL parser to a deeper module' with no user-visible surface. What does /slice's tracer-bullet validator do?
evals.expected_output: Hard gate fails. The slice has no Observable: declaration (no slash command output, cron tick, journal line, /status section, hook stdout/exit-code change, or vault file the user can read). It's a horizontal layer slice. The skill must split it differently or merge it with the slice that completes the end-to-end path before showing the breakdown to the user.
expectations: Identifies that the slice fails the tracer-bullet / Observable hard gate
Mentions that the slice must be split or merged with one that produces an observable surface
Names at least one type of acceptable observable (slash command, journal line, /status section, hook output, etc.)

evals.prompt: Two slices both touch ~/work/brain-os-plugin/install.sh. What does /slice do?
evals.expected_output: Disjoint-files validator flags the overlap as a warn-soft warning, not a blocker. Both slices can still ship — `/impl -p N` (alias `--parallel N`) uses Agent-tool worktree isolation so the file conflict is resolved at merge time. The warning is surfaced so the user can decide whether merging the slices would be cleaner than parallel work.
expectations: Identifies the disjoint-files validator as warn-soft (not a hard gate)
Mentions worktree isolation as the reason (or that /impl parallel handles the conflict)
Mentions surfacing it so the user can decide to merge slices if appropriate

evals.prompt: What labels must each AFK issue carry when filed to sonthanh/ai-brain via /slice?
evals.expected_output: Required: area:<repo> (e.g. area:plugin-brain-os), owner:bot (AFK marker), priority:p1-p4, weight:quick or weight:heavy, status:ready / blocked / backlog. HITL slices carry owner:human instead.
expectations: Mentions area: label (with at least one example)
Mentions owner:bot for AFK (and owner:human for HITL)
Mentions priority: label
Mentions weight: label
Mentions status: label

evals.prompt: If slice B is blocked by slice A, what order does /slice create the GitHub issues in, and why?
evals.expected_output: Topological order — A first, then B. Creating B first would force a placeholder reference (#?) in B's `Blocked by` section because A doesn't have a real issue number yet. Creating A first lets B reference A's actual issue number returned by the central filer (`scripts/gh-tasks/create-task-issue.sh`, which echoes the new issue URL on stdout).
expectations: Says blockers / dependencies first (topological order)
Explains the reason (real issue number vs placeholder, captured from the filer's stdout)

evals.prompt: Where does /slice write its outcome log, and what does the action field capture?
evals.expected_output: {vault}/daily/skill-outcomes/slice.log per skill-spec.md § 11. Action = `slice` (normal grill → parent + child issues path) or `re-slice` (user requested merge/split of issues already filed today). Result = pass if no reactive split / merge happens within 48h, partial if user merged 1–2 slices mid-quiz before approval, fail if breakdown rejected entirely.
expectations: Mentions daily/skill-outcomes/slice.log path
References skill-spec.md § 11 or the standard outcome-log convention
Names slice (and ideally re-slice) as the action verb

evals.prompt: When should you NOT use /slice?
evals.expected_output: Single bug fix (file directly via the central filer `scripts/gh-tasks/create-task-issue.sh`), exploratory grill where decisions aren't settled (finish /grill first — the skill should refuse), the plan IS one slice (file it directly without manufacturing sub-issues), cosmetic / typo / config tweak (direct edit + push, no Pocock pipeline overhead).
expectations: Mentions at least two of: single bug, unsettled grill, one-slice plan, cosmetic / typo / config
Distinguishes /slice from direct issue creation via the central filer or direct edit + push

evals.prompt: User runs /slice on a settled grill-session whose title is `2026-04-26-skill-creator-gold-standard` (a single-skill modification). What slice budget should the skill propose, what HITL/AFK default applies, and how does it open the user-quiz?
evals.expected_output: Maintenance budget — 2-4 slices, not the greenfield default of 5-10. Detection heuristic: grill title contains modify/config/cleanup/refactor/audit/etc → maintenance. HITL/AFK: default to AFK (owner:bot) because the grill is at status:pass and replaces pickup's owner:human gate; mark HITL only if the issue carries an unresolved design question. Quiz opens with: `I detected this as maintenance; proposing N slices. Push back on the budget first if wrong, then on individual slice details.`
expectations: Identifies the slice budget as 2-4 (maintenance), not 5-10 (greenfield default)
Mentions the heuristic (grill title keywords like fix/modify/config/cleanup/etc)
Mentions defaulting to AFK / owner:bot when the grill is at status:pass and the work is skill modification
Mentions opening the quiz with the detected-budget intro line

evals.prompt: A grill closes with all decisions locked and full details in conversation context. Is it acceptable for Claude to skip /slice and file the implementation issues directly via the central filer (`scripts/gh-tasks/create-task-issue.sh`) — or any inline shell that creates issues — without going through the slice user-quiz?
evals.expected_output: No — any direct issue-creation path after a grill closes, even when all details are crystallized in conversation context, counts as bypass per the § Anti-Patterns rule. The user-quiz step (numbered breakdown → user iterates → approved) calibrates slice granularity to user intuition, not LLM intuition. Skipping it produces issues sized to LLM intuition that the user then has to clean up. The correct action is to invoke /slice; centralization on the filer is orthogonal to whether the quiz happened.
expectations: Identifies direct issue creation after a grill close as a bypass / anti-pattern (regardless of which command path)
Mentions the user-quiz step as the reason (slice granularity calibration to user intuition rather than LLM intuition)
Names invoking /slice as the correct action

evals.prompt: A user runs /slice on a settled grill-session. After the PRD is synthesized in conversation context, what is the very next deterministic step BEFORE drafting any child slices, and what does the user see?
evals.expected_output: The skill files a parent story issue on sonthanh/ai-brain with the synthesized PRD as the body — labels `type:plan`, `owner:human`, `status:in-progress`, area label inherited from the grill scope. The body MUST contain the sections: User Story, Settled Decisions, Sub-issues (placeholder checklist `- [ ] TBD — children filed in step 7`), Acceptance, Out of Scope. After creation the skill surfaces the parent issue URL + number to the user and waits for `approve` (proceed to slicing) or `edit <section>` (revise PRD before children are filed). Nothing downstream runs until approval — that is the new PRD-review gate added in Workflow Step 2.5 / 2.7.
expectations: Names filing the parent story issue with the PRD as body BEFORE child slices
Lists the parent-body sections (User Story / Settled Decisions / Sub-issues / Acceptance / Out of Scope)
Mentions the labels type:plan + owner:human + status:in-progress on the parent
Mentions surfacing the parent URL + N and awaiting `approve` / `edit` before children are filed

evals.prompt: What MUST the body of a child issue contain that /slice files under a parent story issue, and how does this differ from a standalone /slice issue body?
evals.expected_output: Each child body adds a `## Parent` section pointing to the parent (e.g. `- ai-brain#145`) and a `## Blocked by` section listing prerequisite sibling issue numbers (e.g. `- ai-brain#148`) — or `None — can start immediately` when no siblings block it. Standard sections (What to build / Acceptance / Files / Observable) are unchanged. The Parent reference makes the child reachable from the parent's checklist (which /impl story reads to drive DAG drainage); Blocked by encodes the topological order so /impl story knows which children are AFK-ready right now.
expectations: Mentions a `## Parent` section pointing to the parent issue (e.g. ai-brain#<N>)
Mentions a `## Blocked by` section listing prerequisite sibling issues (or None)
Mentions /impl story (or DAG drain) reading the parent checklist + Blocked by to order execution

evals.prompt: After all child issues are created, what does /slice do to the parent story issue body, and why is this load-bearing for downstream skills?
evals.expected_output: It edits the parent issue body to replace the placeholder Sub-issues checklist with one line per child: `- [ ] ai-brain#<N> — <child title>`. The `gh issue edit <parent> --body` call rewrites the section in topological order. This is load-bearing because /impl story <parent-N> parses exactly that checklist to discover children, builds the DAG from each child's `## Blocked by`, drains the story, and ticks each line `- [ ]` → `- [x]` on close — closing the loop with a single source of truth. Without the auto-fill, /impl story has nothing to walk and the story can't drain autonomously.
expectations: Says the parent body checklist is auto-filled with child issue numbers + titles
Names the format `- [ ] ai-brain#<N> — <title>`
Mentions /impl story consuming the parent checklist to drive DAG drainage / progress tracking

evals.prompt: After Step 1 loads the grill-session into conversation context, what does Step 1.5 do, and what behavior is required when the audit returns a FLAG verdict on P2?
evals.expected_output: Step 1.5 invokes the `/audit p2` skill via the Skill tool. /audit reads the conversation context Step 1 just loaded and emits a markdown summary table (`| P2 | PASS|FLAG|FAIL | … |`). /slice greps the table for any row matching `^\| P[0-9]+ \| (FAIL|FLAG) \|`. A FLAG verdict aborts the same as FAIL — no override flag, no `--force-no-audit` shortcut, and FLAG is NOT silently rationalized into PASS. The user must re-grill, amend the source, or add an explicit `## P2 — pre-positioning` admission section before re-running /slice. PASS only continues to Step 2.
expectations: Mentions invoking /audit p2 via the Skill tool against conversation context loaded in Step 1
Names FLAG and FAIL as both aborting (ternary collapses to binary)
Mentions deterministic grep on the audit-summary table (no LLM judgment of the verdict)
Names the user recovery options: re-grill, amend source, or add `## P2 — pre-positioning` admission section

evals.prompt: Step 2 synthesizes a PRD draft proposing a new wrapper script around an existing skill (e.g. `audit-gate.ts` wrapping `/audit`). What does Step 2.4 do, and why is the verdict expected to be FAIL or FLAG?
evals.expected_output: Step 2.4 invokes `/audit p1` via the Skill tool against the PRD draft already in conversation context. P1 asks (1) Is this the real problem? (2) Does a solution already exist? (3) What's the simplest version? Wrapping an existing skill with a new script fails (2) — the existing skill IS the mechanism; the gap is enforcement at a seam, not new mechanism. P1 verdict: FAIL or FLAG. /slice surfaces the table verbatim and aborts. User collapses the touch points (one skill edit invoking the existing skill), amends the PRD draft, and re-runs /slice. Calibration drawn from tracker entries: depth:leaf|trunk killed by P1 mid-grill (existing owner:bot|human gate covered 80%), grill review gate collapsed 3 → 1 touch points (existing `## Implementation plan` section IS the touch-points table).
expectations: Mentions invoking /audit p1 via the Skill tool against the PRD draft in conversation context
Names the P1 question that fails (does a solution already exist) when proposing a wrapper around an existing skill
Names FLAG and FAIL as both aborting with no override
Mentions the user recovery: collapse touch points / cite existing mechanism / re-run /slice

evals.prompt: A grill-session for legitimate pre-positioning work (e.g. geo-prep rally point — capability gap surfaced before any incident) hits Step 1.5. P2 fails because there is no historical incident. How does the user proceed without bypassing the gate?
evals.expected_output: The user adds an explicit `## P2 — pre-positioning` admission section to the source grill-session that says: this work is forward-looking, no incident exists, accepting the risk because the cost of waiting until incident exceeds the cost of pre-building. The forced admission preserves rigor (the user IS naming the bet) while unblocking legitimate pre-positioning work. After amending the source, re-run /slice; Step 1.5 re-audits and now sees the explicit acknowledgment, which P2 passes. NOT acceptable: removing the audit invocation, ignoring the abort, adding a bypass flag, or rewriting the why with manufactured incidents.
expectations: Names adding `## P2 — pre-positioning` admission section as the legitimate path
Mentions re-running /slice after amending the source
Names what is NOT acceptable (removing the audit, override flag, manufactured incidents)
Explains why forced admission preserves rigor (user names the bet rather than rubber-stamping)

evals.prompt: User runs /slice on a settled grill. Step 5 quiz approved a breakdown with 3 child drafts; the parent body has 4 AC bullets (AC#1..AC#4); two child drafts carry `## Covers AC` listing AC#1 and AC#3 respectively; the third is a pure-component child with an empty `## Covers AC`. What does Step 2.6 do at the Step 5 → Step 6 boundary, and what does the user see?
evals.expected_output: Step 2.6 fires deterministically before any child issue is filed. It applies the parent-AC regex from `references/ac-coverage-spec.md` § 3.1 to the parent body → parent_ac_set = {1, 2, 3, 4}. It applies the child Covers-AC regex from § 3.2 inside each draft's `## Covers AC` section → covered = {1} ∪ {3} ∪ {} = {1, 3}. uncovered = {2, 4}. Non-empty → ABORT (no fall-through to Step 6, no override flag). User sees a markdown table listing AC#2 and AC#4 with their descriptions (parent-AC regex group 2), plus exactly three user options: (1) add a covering child draft, (2) promote an existing component child draft to live-AC by adding the bullet to its `## Covers AC`, (3) amend parent body to drop the orphan AC bullet (re-grill if scope changes). The pure-component child's empty `## Covers AC` is legal per § 1.1 (asserts intent vs filer-forgot). After the user amends drafts, re-run /slice; the gate fires again — PASS only continues to Step 6.
expectations: Names Step 2.6 as a deterministic gate firing at the Step 5 → Step 6 boundary (after quiz approval, before any child issue is filed)
Cites `references/ac-coverage-spec.md` § 3 for the regexes and explicitly notes the regexes are NOT duplicated in /slice prose
Mentions ABORT with a markdown table of uncovered AC bullets (AC# + description), and that there is no override flag
Lists all three user options on gate fail (add covering child / promote component to live-AC / amend parent body)
Mentions that pure-component children carry an empty `## Covers AC` section per § 1.1 (the empty form is legal and asserts intent)

evals.prompt: User runs `/slice 207` where ai-brain#207 was filed manually (not via `create-task-issue.sh --type plan`) and therefore lacks the `type:plan` label. Step 1 takes the existing-parent-issue-number branch. What MUST Step 1 do after `gh issue view` returns the labels, and why?
evals.expected_output: After `gh issue view` returns labels for the existing parent, Step 1 MUST verify `type:plan` is in the label list. If missing, auto-add it via `gh issue edit <N> -R sonthanh/ai-brain --add-label "type:plan"` and surface a one-line note to the user. Default behavior is auto-add (no prompt), because `/slice`'s entire job is preparing the parent for `/impl story` consumption, and `scripts/run-story.ts` (the orchestrator behind `/impl story`) refuses to drain any parent that lacks `type:plan` (parent #N lacks type:plan label → ERROR). Without this verification, a manually-filed parent will pass through `/slice` (children filed correctly, parent body updated correctly) but downstream `/impl story` will refuse to drain the resulting DAG, requiring the user to manually `gh issue edit --add-label type:plan` to unblock. The new-parent path in Step 2.5 already passes `--type plan` to `create-task-issue.sh`, so this verification is needed only on the existing-parent branch.
expectations: Names verifying `type:plan` is in the parent labels as a required Step 1 action on the existing-parent branch
Mentions auto-adding `type:plan` via `gh issue edit ... --add-label "type:plan"` when missing (default — no prompt)
Cites `/impl story` (or `scripts/run-story.ts`) refusing to drain a parent without `type:plan` as the rationale
Distinguishes the existing-parent branch from the new-parent path (Step 2.5 already attaches `type:plan` via `create-task-issue.sh --type plan`)

evals.prompt: After Step 5 quiz approval, three children get filed via `create-task-issue.sh`. What additional GitHub mutation does Step 6 run per child, and what does the parent's GitHub UI show afterwards?
evals.expected_output: After each `create-task-issue.sh` returns a child URL, Step 6 fetches the child's GraphQL node ID via `gh issue view $CHILD_N --json id --jq .id`, then calls the `addSubIssue` mutation: `gh api graphql -f query='mutation($parent:ID!,$child:ID!){addSubIssue(input:{issueId:$parent,subIssueId:$child}){issue{number}}}' -F parent=$PARENT_NODE_ID -F child=$CHILD_NODE_ID`. The parent node ID was captured once at Step 2.5 right after `transition-status.sh`. Result: parent's GitHub UI populates the native sub-issue panel — progress bar + per-child status badges — and `scripts/run-story.ts viewSubIssues()` (run-story.ts:734, shipped in commit 9cf8ca4) discovers children via the `subIssues` GraphQL query rather than parsing the body checklist.
expectations: Names `addSubIssue` GraphQL mutation as the per-child link step (input: issueId = parent, subIssueId = child)
Mentions parent node ID captured once at Step 2.5 (not re-fetched per child)
Mentions native sub-issue panel becoming the canonical status surface (progress bar + badges)
Cites `viewSubIssues()` / GraphQL `subIssues` query as the orchestrator's discovery channel post-link

evals.prompt: How does Step 7 emit the `## Sub-issues` section after children are filed, and what does it do with the parent's `## Acceptance` bullets?
evals.expected_output: Step 7 substitutes the placeholder Sub-issues section with one bare reference per child in topo order — `- #<child-N> — <child title>` — no checkbox. Native sub-issue panel (populated by Step 6's `addSubIssue`) drives status; the body section is the offline-readable text anchor. Acceptance ticks happen on bullet AC bullets via `tickAcceptance` from #221, not on Sub-issues lines, so the checkbox is gone. Then Step 7 amends `## Acceptance` bullets: parses each child's `## Covers AC` per `references/ac-coverage-spec.md` § 1, builds an AC-to-child mapping, and for any AC# covered by ≥2 children injects `(also covered by #M, #K)` between the requirement and the `[evidence ↗](#issuecomment-PLACEHOLDER)` link (placeholder still points to first child's evidence). Single-cover ACs and pure-component children (empty `## Covers AC`) leave bullets untouched.
expectations: Names list-only Sub-issues template `- #<child-N> — <child title>` (no checkbox) in topo order
Mentions native sub-issue panel as canonical status; body section is offline-readable text anchor
Mentions AC-to-child mapping built from each child's `## Covers AC` (cites spec § 1)
Names multi-cover annotation `(also covered by #M, #K)` injected before the evidence link (placeholder points to first child's evidence comment)
Notes pure-component children (empty `## Covers AC`) contribute nothing to the mapping

evals.prompt: Step 8 prints a summary table of filed children. What MUST follow the table, and why?
evals.expected_output: After the summary table, Step 8 MUST append the canonical next-step line `Next: /impl story <parent-N>`. This surfaces the orchestrator path that drives DAG drainage AND the parent-close trigger (Gate C in `scripts/run-story.ts`). Per-child invocations (`/impl <child-N>` or `/impl auto`) close their child but do NOT auto-close the parent unless the per-child parent-close trigger fires. Recommending per-child as the default leaves stories stuck OPEN after all children close — the failure mode that motivated story #219.
expectations: Names the canonical next-step line `Next: /impl story <parent-N>` as required after the summary table
Cites `/impl story` driving DAG drainage AND the parent-close trigger (Gate C in run-story.ts)
Notes per-child `/impl <N>` does not auto-close the parent without the trigger firing — leaving stories stuck OPEN
References story #219 (or the parent-stuck-OPEN failure mode) as the motivation
